elements.sent-2411-003: Tuning the density of resident electrons or holes in semiconductors through electrostatic doping provides crucial insight into the composition of the many excitonic complexes that are routinely observed as absorption or photoluminescence resonances in optical studies.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, we can change the way these resonances shift and broaden in energy by controlling the quantum numbers (e.g., spin and valley) of the resident carriers with applied magnetic fields and doping levels, and by selecting the quantum numbers of the photoexcited or recombining electron-hole (e-h) pair through optical polarization.
elements.sent-2411-005: Here, we discuss the roles of distinguishability and optimality of excitonic complexes, showing them to be key ingredients that determine the energy shifts and broadening of optical resonances in charge-tunable semiconductors.
elements.sent-2411-006: A distinguishable e-h pair means that the electron and hole undergoing photoexcitation or recombination have quantum numbers that are not shared by any of the resident carriers.
elements.sent-2411-007: An optimal excitonic complex refers to a complex whose particles come with all available quantum numbers of the resident carriers.
elements.sent-2411-008: Based on the carrier density, magnetic field, and light polarization, all optical resonances may be classified as either distinct or indistinct depending on the distinguishability of the e-h pair, and the underlying excitonic complex can be classified as either optimal or suboptimal.
elements.sent-2411-009: The universality of these classifications, inherited from the fundamental Pauli exclusion principle, allows us to understand how optical resonances shift in energy and whether they should broaden as doping is increased.
elements.sent-2411-010: This understanding is supported by conclusive evidence that the broadening and decay of optical resonances cannot be simply attributed to enhanced screening when resident carriers are added to a semiconductor.
elements.sent-2411-011: Finally, applying the classification scheme in either monolayer or moir\'{e heterobilayer semiconductor systems, we relate the energy shift and amplitude of the neutral exciton resonance to the compressibility of the resident carrier gas.}
elements.sent-2411-012: Optical absorption and emission processes in electrostatically-doped semiconductors tell us a great deal about the pervading role of the Pauli exclusion principle.
elements.sent-2411-013: One ramification of the Pauli principle in semiconductors is the quantum-mechanical distinguishability of particles in excitonic complexes in terms of their valley and spin quantum numbers \cite{Jones_NatPhys16,Courtade_PRB17,Chen_NatComm18,Ye_NatComm18,Li_NatComm18,Barbone_NatComm18,Liu_PRL20,Li_NanoLett22 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In addition, the Pauli principle restricts the permissible types of recombination channels of dark excitons and trions \cite{He_NatComm20,Liu_PRL20b,Robert_PRL21,Li_PRB22 , the way hot excitons relax in energy \cite{Honold_PRB89,Eccleston_PRB91,Kochereshko_PRB98,Ramon_PRB03,Shahnazaryan_PRB17,Yang_PRB22 , and how impurities assist recombination processes \cite{Ren_PRB23 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, when it comes to the relation between electrostatic doping and the broadening of excitonic optical resonances, or between electrostatic doping and the energy shifts of these resonances \cite{Wang_NanoLett17,Smolenski_PRL19,Wang_PRX20,Liu_NatComm21,VanTuan_PRL22,Choi_PRB24,Tang_Nature20,Wang_NatMater23 , the Pauli exclusion principle is often given a lesser role and other mechanisms are invoked to explain such physics.}
elements.sent-2411-016: To date, no proposed mechanism can satisfactorily or self-consistently explain the broadening and energy shifts of all optical resonances in charge-tunable semiconductors, including theoretical frameworks based on exciton-polarons \cite{Sidler_NatPhys17,Efimkin_PRB17,Efimkin_PRB18,Fey_PRB20,Huang_PRX23 , correlated trions (i.e. ``tetrons'') \cite{Bronold_PRB00,Suris_PSS01,Esser_pssb01,Koudinov_PRL14,Chang_PRB18,Rana_PRB20,VanTuan_PRB22 , intervalley plasmons \cite{Dery_PRB16,VanTuan_PRX17,VanTuan_PRB19,Dery_PRB16 , or arguments that rely on band filling and screening \cite{Scharf_JPCM19 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: To explain all experimental observations self-consistently and to inform on the essential role played by the Pauli exclusion principle, we propose in this work that all excitonic optical resonances should be classified as being either distinct or indistinct, and that their underlying excitonic complexes should be classified as being either optimal or suboptimal.
elements.sent-2411-018: We will show that this classification scheme provides a useful framework to understand whether and how various excitonic resonances shift and broaden in energy as the charge density is varied.
elements.sent-2411-019: Distinct versus indistinct optical resonances: When a semiconductor is doped with resident electrons in the conduction band (CB) or resident holes in the valence band (VB), there is an important difference between optical transitions that involve distinguishable versus indistinguishable e-h pairs.
elements.sent-2411-020: A distinguishable pair means that the electron and hole have unique quantum numbers that are not shared by any of the resident carriers.
elements.sent-2411-021: For example, when the electron and hole are photoexcited into unoccupied valleys \cite{footnote_numbers .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Distinct (indistinct) optical resonances arise from optical transitions of distinguishable (indistinguishable) e-h pairs.
elements.sent-2411-023: To streamline the discussion in the rest of this work, we will also refer to resident carriers as indistinguishable if they share quantum numbers with the electron or hole in the pair, and as distinguishable otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-024: Optimal versus suboptimal excitonic complexes: We say that an excitonic complex is optimal if there are no resident carriers in the system with which the complex could bind to form a different lower-energy complex with more particles.
elements.sent-2411-025: Since particles with similar quantum numbers cannot be part of the same bound complex \cite{VanTuan_arXiv24b , the electrons (or holes) of an optimal excitonic complex are mutually distinguishable and they include all possible valley-spin species of resident electrons (or holes) at the edge of the CB (VB).}
elements.sent-2411-026: This definition implies that the ground-state excitonic complex is always optimal.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, we will show that other complexes can be optimal as well.
elements.sent-2411-028: We will employ these definitions and present an overarching framework by which one can directly analyze the measured behavior of all optical resonances in charge-tunable semiconductors.
elements.sent-2411-029: Focusing on transition-metal dichalcogenide (TMD) monolayers and moir\'{e heterobilayers by using the experimental findings of Refs.~\cite{Liu_PRL20,Li_NanoLett22,Robert_PRL21,Wang_NanoLett17,Smolenski_PRL19,Wang_PRX20,Liu_NatComm21,VanTuan_PRL22,Choi_PRB24,Tang_Nature20,Wang_NatMater23 , we identify five important understandings and universal observations.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Distinct resonances of optimal excitonic complexes neither broaden nor decay when resident carriers are added to the semiconductor.
elements.sent-2411-031: Examples include certain types of trions in valley-polarized monolayers, 6-particle hexcitons, or intralayer excitons in moir\'{e heterobilayers.}
elements.sent-2411-032: These resonances only show a steady energy redshift that is likely caused by a small difference between renormalizations of the band gap and binding energies.
elements.sent-2411-033: There is no broadening or decay in this case because the absorption or recombination processes do not conflict with Pauli exclusion and therefore there is no need to shake-up the Fermi sea (i.e., to rearrange the resident carriers).
elements.sent-2411-034: Indistinct resonances of optimal excitonic complexes do broaden and slowly shift in energy due to shakeup processes when the charge density increases.
elements.sent-2411-035: Examples include certain trions in zero magnetic field.
elements.sent-2411-036: While Coulomb screening by resident carriers gives rise to the small difference between renormalizations of the band gap and binding energies, we present conclusive evidence that this screening does not lead to the decay and broadening of any resonances in the optical spectra.
elements.sent-2411-037: With increasing charge density, the energy blueshift and broadening of the neutral-exciton absorption resonance are especially strong in the presence of distinguishable resident carriers.
elements.sent-2411-038: Their shakeup leads to a stronger blueshift than the blueshift caused by Pauli blocking (band filling).
elements.sent-2411-039: That is, stronger in comparison to the increase of the optical energy gap when indistinguishable resident carriers occupy low energy states in the photoexcited valley.
elements.sent-2411-040: The energy shift and amplitude of the neutral exciton resonance are related to the compressibility of the resident carrier gas.
elements.sent-2411-041: We explain the unique behavior of this resonance when the resident carriers form an incompressible many-body state, including integer filling of Landau levels in a monolayer subjected to a strong magnetic field or fractional filling of a moir\'{e lattice at certain doping levels.}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Type-B exciton complexes at higher energies
elements.sent-2411-071: Thus far, we have discussed optical transitions originating from the uppermost VBs at $\pm K$, which are the well-known ``type-A'' excitonic resonances that appear on the low-energy side of the optical spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-072: We emphasize that the type-A resonance with lowest energy (the ground state) always belongs to an optimal excitonic complex.
elements.sent-2411-073: However, excitonic resonances also appear at much higher energies when the photoexcited hole originates from the lower of the spin-orbit-split VBs; these are the ``type-B'' exciton complexes.
elements.sent-2411-074: Their energies exceed those of type-A resonances by approximately the spin-orbit splitting of the VBs: $\Delta_v\begin{equation}\,\sim$180~meV in Mo-based TMDs and $\Delta_v\end{equation}\,\sim$450~meV in W-based TMDs \cite{Kormanyos_2DMater15 .}

elements.sent-2411-076: These complexes emerge in hole-rich TMD monolayers (Mo- and W-based) or in electron-rich MoSe$_2$ monolayers when the upper CBs are unpopulated \cite{Choi_PRB24 .}

elements.subsection-2411-004: Suggested origin of the universal energy redshift
elements.sent-2411-116: These data show that distinct resonances of optimal excitonic complexes exhibit a universal energy redshift when the charge density increases, and further, that these resonances neither decay nor broaden.
elements.sent-2411-117: The lack of decay and broadening can be reasoned by noting that a distinguishable e-h pair can bind to resident carriers with all available quantum numbers without having to perturb the distribution of resident carriers that are not part of the optimal complex (Appendix \ref{sec:hex ).}
elements.sent-2411-118: In Sec.~\ref{sec:shakeup , we will discuss why and how shakeup processes broaden the optical resonances in cases that the e-h pair is indistinguishable and/or when the complex is suboptimal.}
elements.sent-2411-119: The universal energy redshift is common to distinct resonances of various optimal complexes.
elements.sent-2411-120: This commonality can be attributed to the small but non-zero difference that exists between band-gap renormalization (BGR) and reduction of the binding energy due to screening by resident carriers (namely, $\Delta E = \Delta E_g - \Delta E_b$).

elements.sent-2411-351: The systematic analysis presented in this work shows that distinguishability and optimality, particularly as they relate to the Pauli exclusion principle, are key factors in determining the energy shift and broadening of various excitonic resonances in electrostatically-doped semiconductor systems.
elements.sent-2411-352: We have corrected common misconceptions, showing that screening by charge particles and band filling are in fact minor reasons for the broadening or energy blueshift of excitonic resonances.
elements.sent-2411-353: The one manifestation of screening is the energy redshift of distinct resonances of optimal excitonic complexes, where Pauli exclusion plays minimal role.
elements.sent-2411-354: The screening leads to a reduction of the bandgap energy and a reduction of the binding energy, where the former is slightly larger than the latter, leading to a small net energy redshift when the charge density increases.
elements.sent-2411-355: This effect is seen through distinct resonances of optimal complexes since their excitation (or recombination) does not involve a shakeup of the resident carrier distribution, and thus, these resonances neither broaden nor decay.
elements.sent-2411-356: Shakeup processes do, however, govern the energy shifts and broadening of indistinct resonances of optimal excitonic complexes.
elements.sent-2411-357: In absorption, these processes break the spatial overlap between distinguishable and indistinguishable resident carriers, allowing the distinguishable carrier to bind to a photoexcited e-h pair.
elements.sent-2411-358: In emission, the shakeup process reunites distinguishable and indistinguishable resident carriers after the recombination.
elements.sent-2411-359: We have self-consistently analyzed the various trends of energy shifts and broadenings of neutral excitons in their ground and excited states.
elements.sent-2411-360: The stronger energy blueshift and broadening of suboptimal excitons originate from a shakeup process that evacuates distinguishable resident carriers from the immediate vicinity of the exciton.
elements.sent-2411-361: The neutral exciton shows resilience in the absence of distinguishable resident carriers (i.e., smaller energy shift, weaker broadening, and stronger amplitude), rendering the exciton an optimal complex, and especially in moir\'{e heterobilayer systems wherein its resonance can also become distinct.}
elements.sent-2411-362: Finally, we have elucidated the intimate connection of the compressibility of the resident carrier gas to the amplitude and energy shift of the neutral exciton resonance.
elements.sent-2411-363: Quantifying and elucidating the details of shakeup processes are interesting directions for future investigations, testing whether collective spin excitations such as magnons in valley-polarized systems or collective charge excitations such as long-wavelength plasmons can take part in these processes.
elements.sent-2411-364: Similarly, further studies are needed to quantify the compressibility of the resident carriers in moir\'{e heterobilayers from the measured energy shift and amplitude of exciton resonances.}
elements.sent-2411-365: Hopefully, the pervading nature of the Pauli exclusion principle along with the ensuing concepts of optimality of excitonic complexes, distinguishability of the e-h pair, distinctiveness of the optical resonances, and compressibility of the resident carrier gas will become the accepted approach to interpret optical studies in electrostatically-doped semiconductors.
